name: Inventory Grid
description: A slot-based interface for viewing, moving, stacking, sorting, equipping, or using items.
design_notes: Inventory grids are repeated-use tools, so clarity and input ergonomics matter more than decorative layout. The grid must handle item size, stacks, drag/drop, controller focus, sorting, filtering, and failure feedback for full or restricted inventories.
design_purpose: Let players manage collected items and equipment efficiently.
Communicate capacity, item categories, stack limits, and restrictions.
Support repeated actions such as sorting, moving, comparing, splitting, and using items.
player_fantasy: The player feels organized and prepared because their items are visible and manageable.

parameters.name: grid_row_count
parameters.description: Number of visible item rows before scrolling or paging.

parameters.name: slot_pixel_size
parameters.description: Base visual size of one inventory slot.

parameters.name: max_stack_display_digits
parameters.description: Maximum stack count width before abbreviation.

parameters.name: drag_commit_threshold_pixels
parameters.description: Pointer movement required before a click becomes drag.

edge_cases: Full inventory, partial stacks, locked slots, quest items, and equipment restrictions need clear feedback.
Controller focus should not get trapped when filtered views hide the currently selected item.
Drag/drop must define what happens when the target slot becomes invalid during multiplayer or sorting updates.
Large item names and localized text should not overflow tooltips or comparison panels.
common_bugs: Client UI moves an item before server validation and duplicates or loses it after correction.
Sorting changes item indices while a tooltip or drag operation still references the old slot.
Stack splitting allows zero, negative, or over-cap quantities.
Grid focus skips disabled slots and becomes unreachable with gamepad navigation.
balancing_notes: Small grids create meaningful scarcity but can make loot-heavy games feel like inventory work.
Sorting and filtering reduce friction without necessarily increasing carrying capacity.
accessibility_notes: Support text scaling, high-contrast rarity indicators, gamepad focus order, and non-drag alternatives.
Do not rely only on item icon art; include names, categories, and comparison text where possible.

implementation_notes.unity: Represent each item as item_instance_id plus stack data, and keep slot coordinates in the inventory model. Treat drag/drop, swap, equip, and stack split as validated transactions before updating uGUI or UI Toolkit views.
implementation_notes.godot: Store item_instance_id, slot Vector2i, and stack count outside Control nodes. Emit a drag transaction from the UI, validate stack limits and slot restrictions in the inventory service, then redraw from accepted state.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use UMG or CommonUI only for presentation; send slot coordinates and item_instance_id through an inventory component or server RPC, then apply server correction for failed moves, splits, or equipment restrictions.
implementation_notes.web: Keep inventory as normalized state keyed by item_instance_id and slot coordinates. Commit drag, split, merge, and sort operations through one reducer or transaction so UI animation never becomes the source of truth.

relationships.reason: Inventory grids provide item slots, stack counts, and capacity checks needed by shop buy and sell transactions.
Recipe crafting needs inventory slot and item instance data to validate ingredient consumption.
Trading uses inventory selection, item locks, and stack splitting before escrow commits.

example_games.title: Resident Evil 4
example_games.note: Uses a grid inventory where item placement and size matter.

example_games.title: Minecraft
example_games.note: Uses slot-based inventory management for items and crafting.
